skill.name: de-slopify
skill.description: Review README files and documentation to remove common AI-writing artifacts while preserving meaning and technical accuracy.
skill.summary: Polishes AI-assisted documentation so it reads more naturally and directly.
skill.author: Dicklesworthstone

security_audit.summary: Static analysis reported many Ruby or shell backtick execution hits, one weak cryptography hit, and two system reconnaissance hits. Review found these are false positives from Markdown code fences, inline examples, and prose in a documentation-editing prompt; no executable code, network access, credential access, or prompt-injection attempt was found.

low_findings.title: False Positive: Markdown Backticks Flagged as Command Execution
low_findings.description: The static analyzer interpreted Markdown code fences and inline examples as Ruby or shell backtick execution. These locations are documentation examples and prompt text, not executable code, and no command injection path is present.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The referenced file is a Markdown skill document, and the flagged regions are fenced examples or prose. No Ruby source, shell interpolation, or runtime command execution API is present.

low_findings.title: False Positive: Weak Cryptography Pattern
low_findings.description: The weak cryptography finding at the frontmatter description is not supported by context. The line describes documentation editing and contains no cryptographic API, hash implementation, key handling, or security-sensitive operation.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Line 3 is plain YAML frontmatter text describing the skill purpose. There is no evidence of a weak cryptographic algorithm or any cryptographic behavior.

low_findings.title: False Positive: System Reconnaissance Pattern
low_findings.description: The system reconnaissance findings point to prose about writing tropes, not host inspection. No evidence found of commands or instructions such as uname, whoami, environment dumping, filesystem enumeration, or network discovery.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited lines are natural-language editing guidance. They do not request collection of system data or execution of reconnaissance commands.

content.value_statement: AI-assisted docs can sound formulaic and distract readers from the technical message. This skill gives Claude, Codex, and Claude Code a focused review process for more natural documentation.
actual_capabilities: Identifies overused em dashes and suggests sentence-level alternatives.
Rewrites formulaic phrases such as 'Here is why' and contrast patterns.
Reviews README files, API docs, blog posts, and public-facing prose.
Preserves technical meaning while improving tone and flow.
Provides before-and-after examples for common documentation issues.
limitations: It is a prompt-only skill and does not include an automated parser or linter.
It focuses on prose style, not code correctness or API behavior.
It requires human review for brand voice, legal wording, and product claims.
It may not fit teams that intentionally use a highly polished marketing tone.

use_cases.title: Clean Up a README Before Release
use_cases.description: Review a project README for AI-style phrasing and produce clearer prose before publishing a release.

use_cases.title: Polish Generated API Documentation
use_cases.description: Turn draft documentation from an AI assistant into direct, reader-focused technical writing.

use_cases.title: Review Product Docs After AI Drafting
use_cases.description: Remove generic phrasing from help content while keeping the original product meaning intact.

prompt_templates.title: Quick Documentation Polish
prompt_templates.scenario: Beginner review of a short paragraph or section
prompt_templates.prompt: Review this documentation text for AI-style phrasing. Replace excessive em dashes, generic lead-ins, and formulaic contrasts. Keep the meaning unchanged.

prompt_templates.title: README Style Pass
prompt_templates.prompt: Read this README carefully and revise prose that sounds generated. Keep technical details accurate, preserve headings, and make the writing direct and natural.

prompt_templates.title: Compare Before and After
prompt_templates.prompt: Edit this documentation for natural tone. Show the original issue, the revised wording, and a brief reason for each meaningful change.

prompt_templates.title: Advanced Voice Preservation
prompt_templates.scenario: Detailed review for team documentation standards
prompt_templates.prompt: Revise this document to remove AI-writing artifacts while preserving our voice. Keep necessary structure, avoid marketing polish, and flag unclear claims separately.

output_examples.input: A README paragraph says the tool is not just fast, it is transformative, then adds a 'Here is why' lead-in.
output_examples.output: The revised paragraph states the tool improves build speed, then explains the performance reason directly without a formulaic setup.

output_examples.input: An API guide uses several em dashes in one paragraph and repeats broad claims about seamless workflows.
output_examples.output: The edited guide splits long sentences, replaces em dashes with commas or periods, and keeps only specific workflow benefits.

output_examples.input: A release note opens with forced enthusiasm and vague statements about an amazing new experience.
output_examples.output: The revised release note starts with the actual feature change and explains the user impact in plain language.

best_practices: Review the full document, because style problems often depend on surrounding context.
Preserve precise technical claims and only change wording that weakens clarity or trust.
Ask for explanations of major edits when documentation has product or compliance implications.
anti_patterns: Do not replace every em dash mechanically when it is the clearest punctuation choice.
Do not remove useful structure such as headings, lists, examples, or warnings.
Do not make technical documentation vague just to sound more casual.

faq.question: Does this skill run code or modify files automatically?
faq.answer: No. It provides editing guidance and prompts for documentation review.

faq.question: What types of documents does it work best on?
faq.answer: It works best on README files, product docs, API guides, blog posts, and public-facing technical prose.

faq.question: Can it detect every AI-written sentence?
faq.answer: No. It identifies common patterns, but final judgment still requires a human reviewer.

faq.question: Will it change technical meaning?
faq.answer: It is designed to preserve meaning, but important changes should still be reviewed by the document owner.

faq.question: Is this only for Claude?
faq.answer: No. The skill is written for Claude, Codex, and Claude Code workflows.

faq.question: Does it replace a style guide?
faq.answer: No. It complements a style guide by targeting common AI-writing artifacts.
